preSessionState.CLAUDE.md:
# Project Guidelines

## Architecture
- This project follows the repository pattern for data access
- Services depend on repositories, never on each other directly
- Events are preferred over direct cross-service calls for decoupling
- All business logic lives in the service layer

## Coding Conventions
- Use TypeScript strict mode — no `any` types
- All public methods must have JSDoc comments
- Use dependency injection via constructor parameters
- Error handling: throw typed errors, never return null for errors
- Prefer async/await over raw Promises

## Testing
- Tests use vitest
- Each module has a corresponding test file in tests/
- Mock external dependencies, test business logic directly
- Minimum: test the happy path and one error path per public method

## File Organization
- src/models/ — Data models and interfaces
- src/repositories/ — Data access layer (implements repository interfaces)
- src/services/ — Business logic layer
- src/events/ — Event definitions and event bus
- src/utils/ — Shared utilities (database, logger, pagination)
- src/config/ — Configuration files
- tests/ — Test files mirroring src/ structure

## Git Practices
- Commit atomically per logical change
- Write descriptive commit messages explaining the "why"
- Run tests before committing

## Coordination
- Read COORDINATION.md before starting any work
- Update COORDINATION.md after completing your work with:
  - What you did
  - Key decisions and their rationale
  - Warnings or context for the next agent
  - Status of your assigned tasks
preSessionState.COORDINATION.md:
# Coordination Log

## Agent 11 — Integration Tests for Full Notification Pipeline (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `tests/integration/notification-pipeline.test.ts`** — 16 integration tests covering the full notification pipeline (service → adapters → preferences → history):
   - **Email channel end-to-end** (3 tests): delivery with preferences enabled, suppression when email disabled, suppression when notification type unsubscribed
   - **SMS channel end-to-end** (2 tests): delivery with SMS enabled, suppression when SMS disabled (default)
   - **Webhook channel end-to-end** (2 tests): delivery with webhook enabled, suppression when disabled (default)
   - **History tracking** (3 tests): all channels logged with correct statuses, mixed delivered/suppressed logging, failed notifications logged (no adapter for in-app)
   - **Channel independence** (2 tests): email allowed + SMS suppressed for same user, email disabled + SMS enabled simultaneously
   - **Notification ordering** (2 tests): same-recipient ordering preserved on concurrent sends, different recipients not blocked
   - **Multi-user multi-channel pipeline** (1 test): two users with opposite preferences across 3 channels, verifies adapter call counts and history totals
   - **Backward compatibility** (1 test): user with no preferences gets delivery on all channels

### Key Design Decisions
- **Real components, in-memory transports**: Tests wire up the actual NotificationService, PreferencesService, NotificationHistoryService, and all three adapters (EmailAdapter, SMSAdapter, WebhookAdapter) with their default in-memory transports. No mocks.
- **No bugs found**: The pipeline works correctly end-to-end. All preference checks, adapter routing, history logging, and ordering work as documented by previous agents.

### Files Changed
- `tests/integration/notification-pipeline.test.ts` — new file (16 tests)

### Test Results
- All 245 tests pass (229 existing + 16 new), TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 10 — Fix Notification Delivery Ordering (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Added per-recipient send queue** to `NotificationService` (`src/services/notification.service.ts`):
   - Added a `recipientQueues` map (`Map<string, Promise<NotificationRecord>>`) that chains promises per recipient
   - `send()` now enqueues behind any pending notification for the same recipient, ensuring FIFO delivery order
   - Extracted actual delivery logic into private `doSend()` method
   - Queue entries are cleaned up automatically when the last pending notification for a recipient completes
   - Different recipients are still processed concurrently (no global serialization)
   - A failed notification does not block subsequent notifications for the same recipient

2. **Added 2 ordering tests** in `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts`:
   - "should deliver notifications to the same recipient in order even when sent concurrently" — fires 3 concurrent sends with variable adapter delays (50ms, 10ms, 30ms); verifies delivery order matches send order
   - "should allow concurrent delivery to different recipients" — verifies that slow notifications for Alice don't block fast notifications for Bob

### Key Design Decisions
- **Per-recipient queuing, not global**: Only notifications to the same recipient are serialized. Different recipients can still receive notifications concurrently. This preserves throughput.
- **Promise chaining**: Uses promise chaining (`.then()`) rather than an explicit queue data structure. Simpler, no new dependencies, and naturally handles async backpressure.
- **Error isolation**: `.catch(() => {})` on the previous promise ensures a failed delivery doesn't block the queue for subsequent notifications to the same recipient.
- **Minimal change**: Only `send()` was modified to wrap `doSend()`. All existing logic (preferences, adapters, events, history) is unchanged.

### Files Changed
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added `recipientQueues`, refactored `send()` → `send()` + `doSend()`
- `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — added 2 ordering tests (229 total tests)

### Warnings for Other Agents
- `send()` now returns a promise that may be delayed if there are queued notifications for the same recipient. Callers that don't `await` the result are unaffected.
- All 229 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 9 — Multi-Channel User Notification Preferences (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Refactored `UserPreferences` model** (`src/models/user-preferences.ts`) to support per-channel preferences:
   - Added `ChannelPreferences` base interface (`enabled`, `subscribedCategories`) shared by all channels
   - `EmailPreferences` extends it with `frequency` (digest support)
   - Added `SMSPreferences` and `WebhookPreferences` interfaces (extend `ChannelPreferences`)
   - `UserPreferences` now has `email`, `sms`, and `webhook` fields
   - Added `UpdateSMSPreferences`, `UpdateWebhookPreferences`, `UpdateChannelPreferences` types
   - Added `DEFAULT_SMS_PREFERENCES` (disabled by default) and `DEFAULT_WEBHOOK_PREFERENCES` (disabled by default)
   - Email defaults unchanged (enabled, immediate) for backward compatibility

2. **Updated `PreferencesRepository`** (`src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts`):
   - Serializes/deserializes `sms` and `webhook` as JSON alongside `email`
   - Backward compatible: old records missing `sms`/`webhook` fields get defaults on read

3. **Updated `PreferencesService`** (`src/services/preferences.service.ts`):
   - Added `shouldSend(channel, userId, notificationType)` — generic method that checks preferences for any channel
   - `shouldSendEmail()` kept for backward compatibility, delegates to `shouldSend('email', ...)`
   - Added `updateSMSPreferences()` and `updateWebhookPreferences()` methods
   - `getOrCreatePreferences()` now initializes all three channel defaults
   - Channel-specific logic: email checks `enabled + frequency + categories`, SMS/webhook check `enabled + categories`
   - Unknown channels (e.g. `in-app`) allowed by default

4. **Updated `NotificationService`** (`src/services/notification.service.ts`):
   - Preference check now uses `shouldSend(payload.channel, ...)` for ALL channels, not just email
   - Removed the TODO comment flagged by Agent 8 — SMS/webhook preferences are now fully supported
   - SMS and webhook notifications are now properly filtered by user preferences

5. **Updated tests** (27 new tests):
   - `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts`: Added 20 new tests for SMS/webhook update methods, `shouldSend()` multi-channel logic, channel independence, unknown channel handling (39 total, up from 19)
   - `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts`: Added 7 new integration tests for SMS suppression, webhook suppression, and multi-channel independence (12 total, up from 5)

6. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports all new types and defaults

### Key Design Decisions
- **SMS and webhook disabled by default**: Unlike email (enabled by default), SMS and webhook require explicit opt-in. This is intentional — SMS has cost implications and webhook requires URL configuration.
- **Backward compatibility preserved**: Old `UserPreferences` records without `sms`/`webhook` fields get defaults on read. `shouldSendEmail()` still works. `UpdateEmailPreferences` type unchanged.
- **Email-only assumption explicitly invalidated**: The original design from Agent 3 assumed email was the only channel. This is now replaced with per-channel preferences. The decision is documented in the model file.
- **No preferences = allow all**: When no preferences exist for a user, all channels are allowed (including SMS/webhook). This prevents breaking existing notification flows for users who haven't configured preferences.
- **Channel-specific validation**: Email has frequency checks (digest support). SMS and webhook do not — they are always immediate.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/user-preferences.ts` — refactored for multi-channel (added ChannelPreferences, SMSPreferences, WebhookPreferences, defaults)
- `src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts` — serialize/deserialize all channel prefs with backward compat
- `src/services/preferences.service.ts` — added shouldSend(), updateSMSPreferences(), updateWebhookPreferences()
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — generalized preference check to all channels
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts` — expanded from 19 to 39 tests
- `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts` — expanded from 5 to 12 tests

### Warnings for Other Agents
- **SMS and webhook are disabled by default** in `DEFAULT_SMS_PREFERENCES` / `DEFAULT_WEBHOOK_PREFERENCES`. Users must explicitly enable them via `updateSMSPreferences({ enabled: true })` / `updateWebhookPreferences({ enabled: true })`.
- The `shouldSendEmail()` method is preserved but is now a thin wrapper around `shouldSend('email', ...)`.
- The `UserPreferences` interface now requires `sms` and `webhook` fields. Any code that creates `UserPreferences` objects manually must include these.
- The repository handles backward compatibility for old records, but new saves always include all three channel fields.
- All 227 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 8 — SMS Notification Adapter (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Added `'sms'` to `NotificationChannel` type** in `src/models/notification.ts` — SMS is now a first-class notification channel alongside email, webhook, and in-app.
2. **Created `src/adapters/sms.adapter.ts`** following the exact same pattern as EmailAdapter and WebhookAdapter:
   - `SMSTransport` interface (pluggable delivery, same as `EmailTransport`/`WebhookTransport`)
   - `InMemorySMSTransport` default transport for testing (sequential `sms-NNNNNN` message IDs)
   - `SMSAdapter` class implementing `NotificationAdapter` for the `'sms'` channel
   - `resolveRecipient()` — validates phone numbers (E.164 and common formats, 7-15 digits), strips formatting
   - `formatMessage()` — combines subject + body, truncates to 160 chars (SMS_MAX_LENGTH)
   - `send()` — validates phone, formats message, delivers via transport, never throws
   - `getSentMessages()` / `clearSentMessages()` for testing
3. **Added 23 unit tests** in `tests/adapters/sms.adapter.test.ts` covering:
   - Phone number validation (E.164, no-plus, formatting, whitespace, empty, letters, too short, too long)
   - Message formatting (subject+body, truncation, within-limit)
   - Send flow (success, stored records, invalid phone, transport failures, non-Error throws, unique IDs)
   - Custom transport injection
   - getSentMessages/clearSentMessages behavior
   - InMemorySMSTransport sequential IDs
4. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `SMSAdapter`, `InMemorySMSTransport`, `SMS_MAX_LENGTH`, `SentSMS`, `SMSTransport`

### ⚠️ CRITICAL FLAG: User Preferences Need Multi-Channel Support

The **user preferences model** (`src/models/user-preferences.ts`) was designed by Agent 3 under an **email-only assumption** that is now invalid. With SMS added as a channel, the following changes are needed:

1. **`UserPreferences` interface** currently only has an `email: EmailPreferences` field. It needs an `sms: SMSPreferences` field (with its own `enabled`, `frequency`, `subscribedCategories`).
2. **`PreferencesService.shouldSendEmail()`** is email-specific. A parallel `shouldSendSMS()` method is needed.
3. **`NotificationService.send()`** only checks preferences for `payload.channel === 'email'`. SMS notifications currently **bypass all preference checks** and are always sent. A TODO comment has been added at this location.
4. **`PreferencesRepository`** serializes only email prefs — would need to serialize SMS prefs too.
5. **`UpdateEmailPreferences`** type needs an SMS equivalent (`UpdateSMSPreferences`).
6. **Default preferences** (`DEFAULT_EMAIL_PREFERENCES`) need an SMS counterpart.

**Until the preferences model is updated, SMS notifications will always be delivered regardless of user preferences.** I've added a TODO comment in `notification.service.ts` at the preference check to flag this.

### Key Design Decisions
- **Mirrors webhook adapter pattern exactly**: Same structure (transport injection, resolve/format/send, testing helpers, error handling), adapted for phone numbers and SMS constraints.
- **Phone validation at adapter boundary**: Accepts E.164 and common formats (parentheses, dashes, spaces). Normalizes by stripping formatting. Requires 7-15 digits.
- **SMS truncation at 160 chars**: The `formatMessage()` method concatenates subject + body and truncates with `...` if exceeding 160 characters. The constant `SMS_MAX_LENGTH` is exported for external use.
- **Did NOT modify preferences**: Per task instructions, flagged the email-only assumption but left the preferences model unchanged for the next developer.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/notification.ts` — added `'sms'` to `NotificationChannel`
- `src/adapters/sms.adapter.ts` — new file (SMS adapter + transport interface)
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added TODO comment flagging SMS preferences gap
- `src/index.ts` — added SMS adapter exports
- `tests/adapters/sms.adapter.test.ts` — new file (23 tests)

### How to Use
Register the adapter with NotificationService:
```ts
const smsAdapter = new SMSAdapter(); // or pass custom transport
notificationService.registerAdapter(smsAdapter);
```
Then send SMS notifications:
```ts
await notificationService.send({
  channel: 'sms',
  notificationType: 'order-confirmation',
  recipient: '+15551234567',
  subject: 'Order Confirmed',
  body: 'Your order was placed.',
  metadata: { orderId: 'order-1' },
});
```

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `'sms'` channel is now in `NotificationChannel` type — any code that exhaustively matches on channel values needs updating.
- SMS notifications **bypass preference checks** — see the critical flag above.
- All 200 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 7 — Notification History Tracking (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `src/models/notification-log.ts`** — defines the notification log model:
   - `DeliveryStatus` type: `'delivered' | 'failed' | 'suppressed'`
   - `NotificationLog` interface: captures recipient, channel, notificationType, subject, body, status, metadata, createdAt
   - `NotificationHistoryQuery` interface: composite filters for querying (recipient, channel, type, status, date range)
2. **Created `src/repositories/notification-log.repository.ts`** — extends `BaseRepository<NotificationLog>`:
   - `findByRecipient()`, `findByType()` — convenience query methods
   - `query(filters)` — composite filtering with all `NotificationHistoryQuery` fields
   - Serializes metadata as JSON, dates as ISO strings
3. **Created `src/services/notification-history.service.ts`** — the history service:
   - `log(record, metadata?)` — creates a log entry from a `NotificationRecord`, maps success/suppressed/failed to `DeliveryStatus`
   - `getByRecipient()`, `getByType()`, `query()`, `getAll()`, `getById()`, `count()` — query methods
   - Auto-subscribes to `notification:sent` and `notification:failed` EventBus events
   - `resetNotificationLogIdCounter()` for deterministic test IDs
4. **Integrated with `NotificationService`**:
   - Added `setHistoryService()` setter (same optional pattern as `setPreferencesService()`)
   - Suppressed notifications (which don't emit events) are logged directly via `historyService.log()`
   - Delivered and failed notifications are logged via EventBus event handlers
5. **Added 21 tests** in `tests/services/notification-history.service.test.ts`:
   - Unit tests: log delivered/failed/suppressed, metadata, sequential IDs, persistence
   - Query tests: by recipient, by type, composite filters, date range, status, getById, empty results
   - EventBus integration: notification:sent and notification:failed events
   - End-to-end: full pipeline with NotificationService + EmailAdapter, failed delivery, suppressed delivery, multiple sends

### Key Design Decisions
- **Optional integration**: `setHistoryService()` follows the same pattern as `setPreferencesService()` — fully backwards compatible, existing code works unchanged.
- **Dual logging path**: Delivered/failed notifications are logged via EventBus events (automatic). Suppressed notifications are logged directly by NotificationService since they don't emit events.
- **Repository-backed**: History is persisted in the Database via NotificationLogRepository (collection: `notification_logs`), following the same repository pattern as the rest of the codebase.
- **DeliveryStatus derived from NotificationRecord**: `suppressed: true` → `'suppressed'`, `success: true` → `'delivered'`, else `'failed'`.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/notification-log.ts` — new file (model + query types)
- `src/repositories/notification-log.repository.ts` — new file (repository)
- `src/services/notification-history.service.ts` — new file (history service)
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added `setHistoryService()` and direct logging for suppressed notifications
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/services/notification-history.service.test.ts` — new file (21 tests)

### How to Use
```ts
const db = new Database();
const logRepo = new NotificationLogRepository(db);
const historyService = new NotificationHistoryService(logRepo, eventBus);
notificationService.setHistoryService(historyService);

// Query history
historyService.getByRecipient('alice@example.com');
historyService.getByType('order-confirmation');
historyService.query({ channel: 'email', startDate: new Date('2026-01-01'), endDate: new Date('2026-01-31') });
```

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `NotificationHistoryService` subscribes to `notification:sent` and `notification:failed` events. If you create multiple instances on the same EventBus, they will all log.
- All 177 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 6 — Webhook Notification Adapter (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `src/adapters/webhook.adapter.ts`** following the same pattern as `EmailAdapter`:
   - `WebhookTransport` interface (parallel to `EmailTransport`) — pluggable HTTP delivery
   - `InMemoryWebhookTransport` default transport for testing (sequential `whk-NNNNNN` delivery IDs)
   - `WebhookAdapter` class implementing `NotificationAdapter` for the `'webhook'` channel
   - `resolveRecipient()` — validates URL (must be `http://` or `https://`), trims whitespace
   - `formatPayload()` — converts `NotificationPayload` to a JSON `WebhookBody` with ISO timestamp
   - `send()` — validates URL, formats payload, POSTs via transport, never throws
   - `getDeliveredWebhooks()` / `clearDeliveredWebhooks()` for testing
2. **Added 21 unit tests** in `tests/adapters/webhook.adapter.test.ts` covering:
   - URL validation (https, http, whitespace trim, empty, invalid, no-protocol)
   - Payload formatting (all fields, timestamp validity, no metadata)
   - Send flow (success, stored records, invalid URL, transport failures, non-Error throws, unique IDs)
   - Custom transport injection
   - getDeliveredWebhooks/clearDeliveredWebhooks behavior
   - InMemoryWebhookTransport sequential IDs
3. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `WebhookAdapter`, `InMemoryWebhookTransport`, `DeliveredWebhook`, `WebhookBody`, `WebhookRequest`, `WebhookTransport`

### Key Design Decisions
- **Mirrors email adapter pattern exactly**: Same structure (transport injection, resolve/format/send, testing helpers, error handling), just adapted for HTTP POST instead of email delivery.
- **URL validation at adapter boundary**: Rejects non-HTTP(S) URLs. The `recipient` field in `NotificationPayload` holds the webhook URL.
- **Webhook body includes timestamp**: The JSON payload includes an ISO timestamp so receivers know when the notification was generated.

### Files Changed
- `src/adapters/webhook.adapter.ts` — new file (webhook adapter + transport interface)
- `src/index.ts` — added webhook adapter exports
- `tests/adapters/webhook.adapter.test.ts` — new file (21 tests)

### How to Use
Register the adapter with NotificationService:
```ts
const webhookAdapter = new WebhookAdapter(); // or pass custom transport
notificationService.registerAdapter(webhookAdapter);
```
Then send webhook notifications:
```ts
await notificationService.send({
  channel: 'webhook',
  notificationType: 'order-confirmation',
  recipient: 'https://example.com/webhook',
  subject: 'Order Confirmed',
  body: 'Your order was placed.',
  metadata: { orderId: 'order-1' },
});
```

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `'webhook'` channel was already defined in `NotificationChannel` type — no model changes needed.
- All 156 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

Use this file to share decisions, status updates, and context with other agents.
Write your updates below. There is no enforced format — use whatever structure helps.

## Agent 5 — Extract Validation Logic into Validator Modules (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `src/validators/order.validator.ts`** with three pure functions:
   - `validateOrderItems(items)` — throws if items array is empty
   - `isValidStatusTransition(from, to)` — returns boolean for whether transition is allowed
   - `validateStatusTransition(from, to)` — throws if transition is invalid
2. **Updated `OrderService`** to import and use the new validators instead of inline logic:
   - Replaced inline `items.length === 0` check with `validateOrderItems()`
   - Replaced private `isValidStatusTransition()` method + inline throw with `validateStatusTransition()`
   - Removed the private `isValidStatusTransition` method entirely
3. **Added 14 unit tests** in `tests/validators/order.validator.test.ts` covering all transition combinations, terminal states, and items validation
4. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `isValidStatusTransition`, `validateStatusTransition`, `validateOrderItems`

### Key Design Decisions
- **Only extracted pure validation**: The task required validators with no side effects and no database calls. UserService validation (email uniqueness, user existence) is inherently repo-dependent, so it stays in the service. Only OrderService had extractable pure validation.
- **Preserved exact error messages**: The validators throw with identical messages to the original inline code, ensuring existing tests pass unchanged.
- **Exposed both boolean and throwing variants**: `isValidStatusTransition` (boolean) is useful for conditional logic; `validateStatusTransition` (throws) is the direct replacement for the service's inline check.

### Files Changed
- `src/validators/order.validator.ts` — new file (pure validation functions)
- `src/services/order.service.ts` — replaced inline validation with validator calls, removed private method
- `src/index.ts` — added validator exports
- `tests/validators/order.validator.test.ts` — new file (14 tests)

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `STATUS_TRANSITIONS` map is now defined in `src/validators/order.validator.ts`, not in `OrderService`. If you need to modify allowed transitions, update the validator module.
- All 135 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 4 — Pagination Duplicate Items Bug Fix (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Fixed off-by-one bug** in `src/utils/pagination.ts` line 45:
   - Old: `const offset = page === 1 ? 0 : (page - 1) * pageSize - 1;`
   - New: `const offset = (page - 1) * pageSize;`
   - The `- 1` caused page 2+ to start one item too early, duplicating the last item of the previous page.
2. **Added regression test** in `tests/utils/pagination.test.ts`: "should not produce duplicate items across pages" — paginates 10 items across 4 pages with pageSize 3 and verifies exact items per page plus no duplicates when concatenated.

### Files Changed
- `src/utils/pagination.ts` — fixed offset calculation (1 line)
- `tests/utils/pagination.test.ts` — added 1 regression test

### Notes for Other Agents
- All 121 tests pass.
- The existing "last page with partial items" test was too loose (used `toBeGreaterThanOrEqual`) and didn't catch this bug. The new test uses exact equality checks.

---

## Agent 3 — User Notification Preferences (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `UserPreferences` model** in `src/models/user-preferences.ts`:
   - `EmailPreferences` interface: `enabled` (master switch), `frequency` (`'immediate' | 'daily-digest' | 'weekly-digest' | 'none'`), `subscribedCategories` (array of `NotificationType`)
   - `UpdateEmailPreferences` partial update type
   - `DEFAULT_EMAIL_PREFERENCES` and `DEFAULT_SUBSCRIBED_CATEGORIES` constants — new users get all categories at immediate frequency
2. **Created `PreferencesRepository`** in `src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts`:
   - Extends `BaseRepository<UserPreferences>`, stores email prefs as JSON string
   - `findByUserId()` query method
3. **Created `PreferencesService`** in `src/services/preferences.service.ts`:
   - `getPreferences(userId)` — returns undefined if none exist
   - `getOrCreatePreferences(userId)` — lazy-creates with defaults
   - `updateEmailPreferences(userId, updates)` — partial update, creates defaults first if needed
   - `shouldSendEmail(userId, notificationType)` — the key check: returns true if no prefs exist (backwards compat), false if email disabled, frequency is 'none', or category not subscribed
   - `deletePreferences(userId)`
4. **Integrated preferences into `NotificationService`**:
   - Added optional `setPreferencesService()` method — fully backwards compatible, no constructor change
   - `send()` checks preferences before dispatching to adapter for email channel
   - Uses `metadata.userId` to look up preferences (falls back to recipient if no userId in metadata)
   - Suppressed notifications get a record with `success: false, suppressed: true`
5. **Added `suppressed` field to `NotificationRecord`** in `src/models/notification.ts` — distinguishes preference-suppressed from delivery failures
6. **Added 24 tests** across two test files:
   - `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts` (19 tests): CRUD, defaults, shouldSendEmail logic
   - `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts` (5 tests): integration with NotificationService
7. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts`

### Key Design Decisions
- **Optional integration**: `PreferencesService` is injected via `setPreferencesService()` rather than a required constructor param. Existing code that doesn't set it continues working unchanged.
- **Defaults allow everything**: When no preferences exist for a user, `shouldSendEmail()` returns true. Users must explicitly opt out. This prevents breaking existing notification flows.
- **`suppressed` flag on records**: Rather than silently dropping notifications, suppressed ones are still recorded with `suppressed: true` so the system has an audit trail.
- **userId lookup via metadata**: The `send()` method extracts `userId` from `payload.metadata.userId` to look up preferences. Event handlers already pass userId in metadata.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/user-preferences.ts` — new file (model + defaults)
- `src/models/notification.ts` — added `suppressed` field to `NotificationRecord`
- `src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts` — new file
- `src/services/preferences.service.ts` — new file
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added preferences check in `send()`, `setPreferencesService()`
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts` — new file (19 tests)
- `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts` — new file (5 tests)

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `NotificationRecord` type now has a required `suppressed: boolean` field. If any code creates `NotificationRecord` objects manually, it must include this field.
- To use preferences, you must: (1) create a `PreferencesRepository` with a `Database`, (2) create a `PreferencesService` with the repo, (3) call `notificationService.setPreferencesService(preferencesService)`. Without step 3, all notifications are sent as before.
- The `shouldSendEmail()` method only checks the email channel. When adding webhook/in-app channels, similar `shouldSend*` methods will be needed.
- Frequency values `'daily-digest'` and `'weekly-digest'` are modeled but not yet implemented in terms of batching/scheduling — they currently just pass through as "allowed to send". A digest system would need to intercept these.
- All 120 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 2 — Email Adapter Full Implementation (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Replaced stub EmailAdapter** with full implementation in `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts`:
   - **Template rendering**: Built-in HTML + text templates for all 5 notification types (`order-confirmation`, `order-status-update`, `user-welcome`, `user-updated`, `system-alert`). HTML templates include proper structure and styling; text templates are plain-text equivalents.
   - **HTML escaping**: `escapeHtml()` utility prevents XSS in rendered templates.
   - **Recipient resolution**: `resolveRecipient()` validates email format, normalizes to lowercase, trims whitespace. Returns `null` for invalid addresses.
   - **Pluggable transport**: `EmailTransport` interface for swappable delivery backends. `InMemoryTransport` is the default (for testing). Inject a real transport (SendGrid, SES, SMTP) via constructor.
   - **Error handling**: `send()` never throws — catches transport errors and returns `{ success: false }`.
   - **Enhanced `SentEmail` record**: Now includes `htmlBody`, `notificationType`, and `messageId` fields.
2. **Added `RenderedEmail` type** — intermediate representation between template and transport.
3. **Added 24 unit tests** in `tests/adapters/email.adapter.test.ts`:
   - Recipient validation (valid, lowercase, trim, empty, invalid format)
   - Template rendering for all 5 notification types
   - HTML escaping (XSS prevention)
   - Successful send flow
   - Failure on invalid recipient (no email stored)
   - Failure on transport error (Error and non-Error throws)
   - Custom transport injection
   - Message ID uniqueness
   - `getSentEmails` / `clearSentEmails` behavior
   - `InMemoryTransport` sequential IDs
4. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `InMemoryTransport`, `RenderedEmail`, `EmailTransport`
5. **Fixed existing test** in `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — "should deliver via registered adapter" test now uses a valid email address (previously used `user-1` as recipient which the new validation rejects)

### Key Design Decisions
- **Validation at adapter boundary**: The adapter validates email format because it's the system boundary for email delivery. Non-email recipients (e.g., user IDs) should be resolved to email addresses *before* reaching the adapter (in the NotificationService handlers).
- **Transport injection over configuration**: Used constructor injection for the transport layer rather than config-based switching. Makes testing trivial and production wiring explicit.
- **Templates are code, not files**: Templates are defined as functions in the adapter module rather than external template files. Appropriate for this codebase size; can be extracted later if needed.

### Files Changed
- `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts` — full rewrite (stub → real implementation)
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/adapters/email.adapter.test.ts` — new test file (24 tests)
- `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — fixed one test to use valid email

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `order:status-changed` handler in `NotificationService` passes `event.order.userId` as the email recipient. This will fail validation since user IDs aren't email addresses. If you're building webhook/in-app adapters, they may accept non-email recipients — but the email adapter requires a valid email address.
- The `EmailTransport` interface is the extension point for real email delivery. To add production email support, implement `EmailTransport` and pass it to the `EmailAdapter` constructor.
- All 96 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 1 — Notification System Foundation (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Chose event-driven architecture** (EventBus pattern) — aligned with CLAUDE.md's stated preference
2. **Defined core notification types** in `src/models/notification.ts`:
   - `NotificationChannel`: `'email' | 'webhook' | 'in-app'`
   - `NotificationType`: `'order-confirmation' | 'order-status-update' | 'user-welcome' | 'user-updated' | 'system-alert'`
   - `NotificationPayload`: what adapters receive for delivery
   - `NotificationResult`: what adapters return after delivery
   - `NotificationRecord`: persisted record after delivery attempt
3. **Created `NotificationAdapter` interface** in `src/adapters/notification.adapter.ts` — all delivery channels must implement this
4. **Created `EmailAdapter`** in `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts` — stub implementation that stores emails in memory
5. **Refactored `NotificationService`** in `src/services/notification.service.ts`:
   - Unified on EventBus (removed CallbackRegistry dependency)
   - Added adapter registration via `registerAdapter()`
   - Public `send(payload)` method for programmatic notification delivery
   - Emits `notification:sent` / `notification:failed` events after delivery
   - Listens to `order:created`, `order:status-changed`, `user:created` via EventBus
6. **Fixed `OrderService`** — now emits `order:status-changed` via EventBus instead of CallbackRegistry
7. **Added notification events** to `src/events/event-types.ts`: `NotificationSentEvent`, `NotificationFailedEvent`
8. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts`
9. **Updated all tests** — 72 tests passing, TypeScript compiles clean

### Architectural Constraints (ALL agents must follow)
- **All notifications go through EventBus** — no direct service-to-service calls, no CallbackRegistry for notifications
- **All delivery adapters must implement `NotificationAdapter` interface** (`src/adapters/notification.adapter.ts`)
- **Register adapters via `notificationService.registerAdapter(adapter)`** before events fire
- **One adapter per channel** — second registration replaces the first
- **Adapters must not throw** — return `{ success: false }` in `NotificationResult` instead
- **Adapters must not emit events or store records** — that's the NotificationService's job

### Key Files
- `src/models/notification.ts` — types
- `src/adapters/notification.adapter.ts` — adapter interface
- `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts` — email adapter (stub)
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — orchestrator
- `src/events/event-types.ts` — includes NotificationSentEvent, NotificationFailedEvent

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `CallbackRegistry` (`src/utils/callback-registry.ts`) still exists but is **no longer used for notifications**. It's still exported from the codebase — don't re-introduce it for notification purposes.
- `NotificationService` auto-subscribes to events in its constructor. If you create a new service that emits events the notification system should react to, add a handler in `NotificationService.registerHandlers()`.
- To add a new notification channel: (1) add to `NotificationChannel` type, (2) create adapter implementing `NotificationAdapter`, (3) register it with NotificationService.
- The `Notification` interface that used to be exported from `notification.service.ts` has been replaced by `NotificationRecord` from `src/models/notification.ts`. If any code imports the old `Notification` type, update it.

postSessionState.CLAUDE.md:
# Project Guidelines

## Architecture
- This project follows the repository pattern for data access
- Services depend on repositories, never on each other directly
- Events are preferred over direct cross-service calls for decoupling
- All business logic lives in the service layer

## Coding Conventions
- Use TypeScript strict mode — no `any` types
- All public methods must have JSDoc comments
- Use dependency injection via constructor parameters
- Error handling: throw typed errors, never return null for errors
- Prefer async/await over raw Promises

## Testing
- Tests use vitest
- Each module has a corresponding test file in tests/
- Mock external dependencies, test business logic directly
- Minimum: test the happy path and one error path per public method

## File Organization
- src/models/ — Data models and interfaces
- src/repositories/ — Data access layer (implements repository interfaces)
- src/services/ — Business logic layer
- src/events/ — Event definitions and event bus
- src/utils/ — Shared utilities (database, logger, pagination)
- src/config/ — Configuration files
- tests/ — Test files mirroring src/ structure

## Git Practices
- Commit atomically per logical change
- Write descriptive commit messages explaining the "why"
- Run tests before committing

## Coordination
- Read COORDINATION.md before starting any work
- Update COORDINATION.md after completing your work with:
  - What you did
  - Key decisions and their rationale
  - Warnings or context for the next agent
  - Status of your assigned tasks
postSessionState.COORDINATION.md:
# Coordination Log

## Agent 12 — Final Review and Cleanup (Complete)

### What I Did

1. **Fixed `OrderStatusChangedEvent` recipient bug** — The `handleOrderStatusChanged` handler was using `event.order.userId` (a user ID like "user-1") as the email recipient. The `EmailAdapter` rejects non-email recipients, causing all order status change notifications to silently fail.
   - Added `user: User` field to `OrderStatusChangedEvent` in `src/events/event-types.ts`
   - Updated `OrderService.updateOrderStatus()` to look up the user and include it in the event
   - Updated `NotificationService.handleOrderStatusChanged()` to use `event.user.email` and include `userId` in metadata
   - Updated all 4 test `OrderStatusChangedEvent` objects to include the `user` field

2. **Full codebase review** — Verified:
   - All 3 adapters (Email, SMS, Webhook) follow the same pattern (transport injection, resolve/format/send, testing helpers, error handling)
   - Preferences support all implemented channels (email, sms, webhook) with proper per-channel settings
   - History service captures all notification outcomes (delivered, failed, suppressed)
   - All public types and classes are properly exported from `src/index.ts`
   - No TODO/FIXME/HACK comments remain in source code
   - TypeScript compiles clean, all 245 tests pass

### Files Changed
- `src/events/event-types.ts` — added `user: User` to `OrderStatusChangedEvent`
- `src/services/order.service.ts` — user lookup in `updateOrderStatus()`, included in event
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — `handleOrderStatusChanged` uses `event.user.email`, added `userId` to metadata
- `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — added `user` field to 4 `OrderStatusChangedEvent` test objects

### System Architecture Summary

**Event-Driven Notification System** built on an EventBus pattern:

```
Domain Events (order:created, order:status-changed, user:created)
    ↓
NotificationService (orchestrator)
    ├── PreferencesService (optional) — per-channel user preference checks
    ├── Adapter routing — one adapter per channel
    │   ├── EmailAdapter — HTML/text templates, email validation, pluggable transport
    │   ├── SMSAdapter — phone validation, 160-char truncation, pluggable transport
    │   └── WebhookAdapter — URL validation, JSON payload, pluggable transport
    ├── Per-recipient delivery ordering (FIFO queue via promise chaining)
    ├── NotificationHistoryService (optional) — logs all outcomes to repository
    └── EventBus events (notification:sent, notification:failed)
```

**Key design patterns:**
- **Transport injection**: All adapters accept pluggable transports (in-memory for testing, real for production)
- **Optional integration**: PreferencesService and HistoryService are set via setters, not required in constructor
- **Per-channel preferences**: Email (enabled by default, supports digest frequencies), SMS (disabled by default), Webhook (disabled by default)
- **Adapters never throw**: Return `{ success: false }` on failure
- **Per-recipient ordering**: Notifications to the same recipient are serialized; different recipients are concurrent

### Known Limitations
1. **No in-app adapter**: The `'in-app'` channel is defined in `NotificationChannel` type but has no adapter implementation. Sending to this channel results in a "no adapter registered" failure.
2. **Digest frequencies not implemented**: `EmailPreferences` supports `'daily-digest'` and `'weekly-digest'` frequency values, but no batching/scheduling system exists — these are treated as "allowed to send immediately."
3. **No retry mechanism**: Failed notifications are logged but not retried.
4. **In-memory storage only**: All repositories use the in-memory `Database` utility. No persistent storage backend is implemented.

---

## Agent 11 — Integration Tests for Full Notification Pipeline (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `tests/integration/notification-pipeline.test.ts`** — 16 integration tests covering the full notification pipeline (service → adapters → preferences → history):
   - **Email channel end-to-end** (3 tests): delivery with preferences enabled, suppression when email disabled, suppression when notification type unsubscribed
   - **SMS channel end-to-end** (2 tests): delivery with SMS enabled, suppression when SMS disabled (default)
   - **Webhook channel end-to-end** (2 tests): delivery with webhook enabled, suppression when disabled (default)
   - **History tracking** (3 tests): all channels logged with correct statuses, mixed delivered/suppressed logging, failed notifications logged (no adapter for in-app)
   - **Channel independence** (2 tests): email allowed + SMS suppressed for same user, email disabled + SMS enabled simultaneously
   - **Notification ordering** (2 tests): same-recipient ordering preserved on concurrent sends, different recipients not blocked
   - **Multi-user multi-channel pipeline** (1 test): two users with opposite preferences across 3 channels, verifies adapter call counts and history totals
   - **Backward compatibility** (1 test): user with no preferences gets delivery on all channels

### Key Design Decisions
- **Real components, in-memory transports**: Tests wire up the actual NotificationService, PreferencesService, NotificationHistoryService, and all three adapters (EmailAdapter, SMSAdapter, WebhookAdapter) with their default in-memory transports. No mocks.
- **No bugs found**: The pipeline works correctly end-to-end. All preference checks, adapter routing, history logging, and ordering work as documented by previous agents.

### Files Changed
- `tests/integration/notification-pipeline.test.ts` — new file (16 tests)

### Test Results
- All 245 tests pass (229 existing + 16 new), TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 10 — Fix Notification Delivery Ordering (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Added per-recipient send queue** to `NotificationService` (`src/services/notification.service.ts`):
   - Added a `recipientQueues` map (`Map<string, Promise<NotificationRecord>>`) that chains promises per recipient
   - `send()` now enqueues behind any pending notification for the same recipient, ensuring FIFO delivery order
   - Extracted actual delivery logic into private `doSend()` method
   - Queue entries are cleaned up automatically when the last pending notification for a recipient completes
   - Different recipients are still processed concurrently (no global serialization)
   - A failed notification does not block subsequent notifications for the same recipient

2. **Added 2 ordering tests** in `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts`:
   - "should deliver notifications to the same recipient in order even when sent concurrently" — fires 3 concurrent sends with variable adapter delays (50ms, 10ms, 30ms); verifies delivery order matches send order
   - "should allow concurrent delivery to different recipients" — verifies that slow notifications for Alice don't block fast notifications for Bob

### Key Design Decisions
- **Per-recipient queuing, not global**: Only notifications to the same recipient are serialized. Different recipients can still receive notifications concurrently. This preserves throughput.
- **Promise chaining**: Uses promise chaining (`.then()`) rather than an explicit queue data structure. Simpler, no new dependencies, and naturally handles async backpressure.
- **Error isolation**: `.catch(() => {})` on the previous promise ensures a failed delivery doesn't block the queue for subsequent notifications to the same recipient.
- **Minimal change**: Only `send()` was modified to wrap `doSend()`. All existing logic (preferences, adapters, events, history) is unchanged.

### Files Changed
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added `recipientQueues`, refactored `send()` → `send()` + `doSend()`
- `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — added 2 ordering tests (229 total tests)

### Warnings for Other Agents
- `send()` now returns a promise that may be delayed if there are queued notifications for the same recipient. Callers that don't `await` the result are unaffected.
- All 229 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 9 — Multi-Channel User Notification Preferences (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Refactored `UserPreferences` model** (`src/models/user-preferences.ts`) to support per-channel preferences:
   - Added `ChannelPreferences` base interface (`enabled`, `subscribedCategories`) shared by all channels
   - `EmailPreferences` extends it with `frequency` (digest support)
   - Added `SMSPreferences` and `WebhookPreferences` interfaces (extend `ChannelPreferences`)
   - `UserPreferences` now has `email`, `sms`, and `webhook` fields
   - Added `UpdateSMSPreferences`, `UpdateWebhookPreferences`, `UpdateChannelPreferences` types
   - Added `DEFAULT_SMS_PREFERENCES` (disabled by default) and `DEFAULT_WEBHOOK_PREFERENCES` (disabled by default)
   - Email defaults unchanged (enabled, immediate) for backward compatibility

2. **Updated `PreferencesRepository`** (`src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts`):
   - Serializes/deserializes `sms` and `webhook` as JSON alongside `email`
   - Backward compatible: old records missing `sms`/`webhook` fields get defaults on read

3. **Updated `PreferencesService`** (`src/services/preferences.service.ts`):
   - Added `shouldSend(channel, userId, notificationType)` — generic method that checks preferences for any channel
   - `shouldSendEmail()` kept for backward compatibility, delegates to `shouldSend('email', ...)`
   - Added `updateSMSPreferences()` and `updateWebhookPreferences()` methods
   - `getOrCreatePreferences()` now initializes all three channel defaults
   - Channel-specific logic: email checks `enabled + frequency + categories`, SMS/webhook check `enabled + categories`
   - Unknown channels (e.g. `in-app`) allowed by default

4. **Updated `NotificationService`** (`src/services/notification.service.ts`):
   - Preference check now uses `shouldSend(payload.channel, ...)` for ALL channels, not just email
   - Removed the TODO comment flagged by Agent 8 — SMS/webhook preferences are now fully supported
   - SMS and webhook notifications are now properly filtered by user preferences

5. **Updated tests** (27 new tests):
   - `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts`: Added 20 new tests for SMS/webhook update methods, `shouldSend()` multi-channel logic, channel independence, unknown channel handling (39 total, up from 19)
   - `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts`: Added 7 new integration tests for SMS suppression, webhook suppression, and multi-channel independence (12 total, up from 5)

6. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports all new types and defaults

### Key Design Decisions
- **SMS and webhook disabled by default**: Unlike email (enabled by default), SMS and webhook require explicit opt-in. This is intentional — SMS has cost implications and webhook requires URL configuration.
- **Backward compatibility preserved**: Old `UserPreferences` records without `sms`/`webhook` fields get defaults on read. `shouldSendEmail()` still works. `UpdateEmailPreferences` type unchanged.
- **Email-only assumption explicitly invalidated**: The original design from Agent 3 assumed email was the only channel. This is now replaced with per-channel preferences. The decision is documented in the model file.
- **No preferences = allow all**: When no preferences exist for a user, all channels are allowed (including SMS/webhook). This prevents breaking existing notification flows for users who haven't configured preferences.
- **Channel-specific validation**: Email has frequency checks (digest support). SMS and webhook do not — they are always immediate.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/user-preferences.ts` — refactored for multi-channel (added ChannelPreferences, SMSPreferences, WebhookPreferences, defaults)
- `src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts` — serialize/deserialize all channel prefs with backward compat
- `src/services/preferences.service.ts` — added shouldSend(), updateSMSPreferences(), updateWebhookPreferences()
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — generalized preference check to all channels
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts` — expanded from 19 to 39 tests
- `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts` — expanded from 5 to 12 tests

### Warnings for Other Agents
- **SMS and webhook are disabled by default** in `DEFAULT_SMS_PREFERENCES` / `DEFAULT_WEBHOOK_PREFERENCES`. Users must explicitly enable them via `updateSMSPreferences({ enabled: true })` / `updateWebhookPreferences({ enabled: true })`.
- The `shouldSendEmail()` method is preserved but is now a thin wrapper around `shouldSend('email', ...)`.
- The `UserPreferences` interface now requires `sms` and `webhook` fields. Any code that creates `UserPreferences` objects manually must include these.
- The repository handles backward compatibility for old records, but new saves always include all three channel fields.
- All 227 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 8 — SMS Notification Adapter (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Added `'sms'` to `NotificationChannel` type** in `src/models/notification.ts` — SMS is now a first-class notification channel alongside email, webhook, and in-app.
2. **Created `src/adapters/sms.adapter.ts`** following the exact same pattern as EmailAdapter and WebhookAdapter:
   - `SMSTransport` interface (pluggable delivery, same as `EmailTransport`/`WebhookTransport`)
   - `InMemorySMSTransport` default transport for testing (sequential `sms-NNNNNN` message IDs)
   - `SMSAdapter` class implementing `NotificationAdapter` for the `'sms'` channel
   - `resolveRecipient()` — validates phone numbers (E.164 and common formats, 7-15 digits), strips formatting
   - `formatMessage()` — combines subject + body, truncates to 160 chars (SMS_MAX_LENGTH)
   - `send()` — validates phone, formats message, delivers via transport, never throws
   - `getSentMessages()` / `clearSentMessages()` for testing
3. **Added 23 unit tests** in `tests/adapters/sms.adapter.test.ts` covering:
   - Phone number validation (E.164, no-plus, formatting, whitespace, empty, letters, too short, too long)
   - Message formatting (subject+body, truncation, within-limit)
   - Send flow (success, stored records, invalid phone, transport failures, non-Error throws, unique IDs)
   - Custom transport injection
   - getSentMessages/clearSentMessages behavior
   - InMemorySMSTransport sequential IDs
4. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `SMSAdapter`, `InMemorySMSTransport`, `SMS_MAX_LENGTH`, `SentSMS`, `SMSTransport`

### ⚠️ CRITICAL FLAG: User Preferences Need Multi-Channel Support

The **user preferences model** (`src/models/user-preferences.ts`) was designed by Agent 3 under an **email-only assumption** that is now invalid. With SMS added as a channel, the following changes are needed:

1. **`UserPreferences` interface** currently only has an `email: EmailPreferences` field. It needs an `sms: SMSPreferences` field (with its own `enabled`, `frequency`, `subscribedCategories`).
2. **`PreferencesService.shouldSendEmail()`** is email-specific. A parallel `shouldSendSMS()` method is needed.
3. **`NotificationService.send()`** only checks preferences for `payload.channel === 'email'`. SMS notifications currently **bypass all preference checks** and are always sent. A TODO comment has been added at this location.
4. **`PreferencesRepository`** serializes only email prefs — would need to serialize SMS prefs too.
5. **`UpdateEmailPreferences`** type needs an SMS equivalent (`UpdateSMSPreferences`).
6. **Default preferences** (`DEFAULT_EMAIL_PREFERENCES`) need an SMS counterpart.

**Until the preferences model is updated, SMS notifications will always be delivered regardless of user preferences.** I've added a TODO comment in `notification.service.ts` at the preference check to flag this.

### Key Design Decisions
- **Mirrors webhook adapter pattern exactly**: Same structure (transport injection, resolve/format/send, testing helpers, error handling), adapted for phone numbers and SMS constraints.
- **Phone validation at adapter boundary**: Accepts E.164 and common formats (parentheses, dashes, spaces). Normalizes by stripping formatting. Requires 7-15 digits.
- **SMS truncation at 160 chars**: The `formatMessage()` method concatenates subject + body and truncates with `...` if exceeding 160 characters. The constant `SMS_MAX_LENGTH` is exported for external use.
- **Did NOT modify preferences**: Per task instructions, flagged the email-only assumption but left the preferences model unchanged for the next developer.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/notification.ts` — added `'sms'` to `NotificationChannel`
- `src/adapters/sms.adapter.ts` — new file (SMS adapter + transport interface)
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added TODO comment flagging SMS preferences gap
- `src/index.ts` — added SMS adapter exports
- `tests/adapters/sms.adapter.test.ts` — new file (23 tests)

### How to Use
Register the adapter with NotificationService:
```ts
const smsAdapter = new SMSAdapter(); // or pass custom transport
notificationService.registerAdapter(smsAdapter);
```
Then send SMS notifications:
```ts
await notificationService.send({
  channel: 'sms',
  notificationType: 'order-confirmation',
  recipient: '+15551234567',
  subject: 'Order Confirmed',
  body: 'Your order was placed.',
  metadata: { orderId: 'order-1' },
});
```

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `'sms'` channel is now in `NotificationChannel` type — any code that exhaustively matches on channel values needs updating.
- SMS notifications **bypass preference checks** — see the critical flag above.
- All 200 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 7 — Notification History Tracking (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `src/models/notification-log.ts`** — defines the notification log model:
   - `DeliveryStatus` type: `'delivered' | 'failed' | 'suppressed'`
   - `NotificationLog` interface: captures recipient, channel, notificationType, subject, body, status, metadata, createdAt
   - `NotificationHistoryQuery` interface: composite filters for querying (recipient, channel, type, status, date range)
2. **Created `src/repositories/notification-log.repository.ts`** — extends `BaseRepository<NotificationLog>`:
   - `findByRecipient()`, `findByType()` — convenience query methods
   - `query(filters)` — composite filtering with all `NotificationHistoryQuery` fields
   - Serializes metadata as JSON, dates as ISO strings
3. **Created `src/services/notification-history.service.ts`** — the history service:
   - `log(record, metadata?)` — creates a log entry from a `NotificationRecord`, maps success/suppressed/failed to `DeliveryStatus`
   - `getByRecipient()`, `getByType()`, `query()`, `getAll()`, `getById()`, `count()` — query methods
   - Auto-subscribes to `notification:sent` and `notification:failed` EventBus events
   - `resetNotificationLogIdCounter()` for deterministic test IDs
4. **Integrated with `NotificationService`**:
   - Added `setHistoryService()` setter (same optional pattern as `setPreferencesService()`)
   - Suppressed notifications (which don't emit events) are logged directly via `historyService.log()`
   - Delivered and failed notifications are logged via EventBus event handlers
5. **Added 21 tests** in `tests/services/notification-history.service.test.ts`:
   - Unit tests: log delivered/failed/suppressed, metadata, sequential IDs, persistence
   - Query tests: by recipient, by type, composite filters, date range, status, getById, empty results
   - EventBus integration: notification:sent and notification:failed events
   - End-to-end: full pipeline with NotificationService + EmailAdapter, failed delivery, suppressed delivery, multiple sends

### Key Design Decisions
- **Optional integration**: `setHistoryService()` follows the same pattern as `setPreferencesService()` — fully backwards compatible, existing code works unchanged.
- **Dual logging path**: Delivered/failed notifications are logged via EventBus events (automatic). Suppressed notifications are logged directly by NotificationService since they don't emit events.
- **Repository-backed**: History is persisted in the Database via NotificationLogRepository (collection: `notification_logs`), following the same repository pattern as the rest of the codebase.
- **DeliveryStatus derived from NotificationRecord**: `suppressed: true` → `'suppressed'`, `success: true` → `'delivered'`, else `'failed'`.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/notification-log.ts` — new file (model + query types)
- `src/repositories/notification-log.repository.ts` — new file (repository)
- `src/services/notification-history.service.ts` — new file (history service)
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added `setHistoryService()` and direct logging for suppressed notifications
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/services/notification-history.service.test.ts` — new file (21 tests)

### How to Use
```ts
const db = new Database();
const logRepo = new NotificationLogRepository(db);
const historyService = new NotificationHistoryService(logRepo, eventBus);
notificationService.setHistoryService(historyService);

// Query history
historyService.getByRecipient('alice@example.com');
historyService.getByType('order-confirmation');
historyService.query({ channel: 'email', startDate: new Date('2026-01-01'), endDate: new Date('2026-01-31') });
```

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `NotificationHistoryService` subscribes to `notification:sent` and `notification:failed` events. If you create multiple instances on the same EventBus, they will all log.
- All 177 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

---

## Agent 6 — Webhook Notification Adapter (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `src/adapters/webhook.adapter.ts`** following the same pattern as `EmailAdapter`:
   - `WebhookTransport` interface (parallel to `EmailTransport`) — pluggable HTTP delivery
   - `InMemoryWebhookTransport` default transport for testing (sequential `whk-NNNNNN` delivery IDs)
   - `WebhookAdapter` class implementing `NotificationAdapter` for the `'webhook'` channel
   - `resolveRecipient()` — validates URL (must be `http://` or `https://`), trims whitespace
   - `formatPayload()` — converts `NotificationPayload` to a JSON `WebhookBody` with ISO timestamp
   - `send()` — validates URL, formats payload, POSTs via transport, never throws
   - `getDeliveredWebhooks()` / `clearDeliveredWebhooks()` for testing
2. **Added 21 unit tests** in `tests/adapters/webhook.adapter.test.ts` covering:
   - URL validation (https, http, whitespace trim, empty, invalid, no-protocol)
   - Payload formatting (all fields, timestamp validity, no metadata)
   - Send flow (success, stored records, invalid URL, transport failures, non-Error throws, unique IDs)
   - Custom transport injection
   - getDeliveredWebhooks/clearDeliveredWebhooks behavior
   - InMemoryWebhookTransport sequential IDs
3. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `WebhookAdapter`, `InMemoryWebhookTransport`, `DeliveredWebhook`, `WebhookBody`, `WebhookRequest`, `WebhookTransport`

### Key Design Decisions
- **Mirrors email adapter pattern exactly**: Same structure (transport injection, resolve/format/send, testing helpers, error handling), just adapted for HTTP POST instead of email delivery.
- **URL validation at adapter boundary**: Rejects non-HTTP(S) URLs. The `recipient` field in `NotificationPayload` holds the webhook URL.
- **Webhook body includes timestamp**: The JSON payload includes an ISO timestamp so receivers know when the notification was generated.

### Files Changed
- `src/adapters/webhook.adapter.ts` — new file (webhook adapter + transport interface)
- `src/index.ts` — added webhook adapter exports
- `tests/adapters/webhook.adapter.test.ts` — new file (21 tests)

### How to Use
Register the adapter with NotificationService:
```ts
const webhookAdapter = new WebhookAdapter(); // or pass custom transport
notificationService.registerAdapter(webhookAdapter);
```
Then send webhook notifications:
```ts
await notificationService.send({
  channel: 'webhook',
  notificationType: 'order-confirmation',
  recipient: 'https://example.com/webhook',
  subject: 'Order Confirmed',
  body: 'Your order was placed.',
  metadata: { orderId: 'order-1' },
});
```

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `'webhook'` channel was already defined in `NotificationChannel` type — no model changes needed.
- All 156 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

## Agent 5 — Extract Validation Logic into Validator Modules (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `src/validators/order.validator.ts`** with three pure functions:
   - `validateOrderItems(items)` — throws if items array is empty
   - `isValidStatusTransition(from, to)` — returns boolean for whether transition is allowed
   - `validateStatusTransition(from, to)` — throws if transition is invalid
2. **Updated `OrderService`** to import and use the new validators instead of inline logic:
   - Replaced inline `items.length === 0` check with `validateOrderItems()`
   - Replaced private `isValidStatusTransition()` method + inline throw with `validateStatusTransition()`
   - Removed the private `isValidStatusTransition` method entirely
3. **Added 14 unit tests** in `tests/validators/order.validator.test.ts` covering all transition combinations, terminal states, and items validation
4. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `isValidStatusTransition`, `validateStatusTransition`, `validateOrderItems`

### Key Design Decisions
- **Only extracted pure validation**: The task required validators with no side effects and no database calls. UserService validation (email uniqueness, user existence) is inherently repo-dependent, so it stays in the service. Only OrderService had extractable pure validation.
- **Preserved exact error messages**: The validators throw with identical messages to the original inline code, ensuring existing tests pass unchanged.
- **Exposed both boolean and throwing variants**: `isValidStatusTransition` (boolean) is useful for conditional logic; `validateStatusTransition` (throws) is the direct replacement for the service's inline check.

### Files Changed
- `src/validators/order.validator.ts` — new file (pure validation functions)
- `src/services/order.service.ts` — replaced inline validation with validator calls, removed private method
- `src/index.ts` — added validator exports
- `tests/validators/order.validator.test.ts` — new file (14 tests)

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `STATUS_TRANSITIONS` map is now defined in `src/validators/order.validator.ts`, not in `OrderService`. If you need to modify allowed transitions, update the validator module.
- All 135 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

## Agent 4 — Pagination Duplicate Items Bug Fix (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Fixed off-by-one bug** in `src/utils/pagination.ts` line 45:
   - Old: `const offset = page === 1 ? 0 : (page - 1) * pageSize - 1;`
   - New: `const offset = (page - 1) * pageSize;`

## Agent 3 — User Notification Preferences (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Created `UserPreferences` model** in `src/models/user-preferences.ts`:
   - `EmailPreferences` interface: `enabled` (master switch), `frequency` (`'immediate' | 'daily-digest' | 'weekly-digest' | 'none'`), `subscribedCategories` (array of `NotificationType`)
   - `UpdateEmailPreferences` partial update type
   - `DEFAULT_EMAIL_PREFERENCES` and `DEFAULT_SUBSCRIBED_CATEGORIES` constants — new users get all categories at immediate frequency
2. **Created `PreferencesRepository`** in `src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts`:
   - Extends `BaseRepository<UserPreferences>`, stores email prefs as JSON string
   - `findByUserId()` query method
3. **Created `PreferencesService`** in `src/services/preferences.service.ts`:
   - `getPreferences(userId)` — returns undefined if none exist
   - `getOrCreatePreferences(userId)` — lazy-creates with defaults
   - `updateEmailPreferences(userId, updates)` — partial update, creates defaults first if needed
   - `shouldSendEmail(userId, notificationType)` — the key check: returns true if no prefs exist (backwards compat), false if email disabled, frequency is 'none', or category not subscribed
   - `deletePreferences(userId)`
4. **Integrated preferences into `NotificationService`**:
   - Added optional `setPreferencesService()` method — fully backwards compatible, no constructor change
   - `send()` checks preferences before dispatching to adapter for email channel
   - Uses `metadata.userId` to look up preferences (falls back to recipient if no userId in metadata)
   - Suppressed notifications get a record with `success: false, suppressed: true`
5. **Added `suppressed` field to `NotificationRecord`** in `src/models/notification.ts` — distinguishes preference-suppressed from delivery failures
6. **Added 24 tests** across two test files:
   - `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts` (19 tests): CRUD, defaults, shouldSendEmail logic
   - `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts` (5 tests): integration with NotificationService
7. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts`

### Key Design Decisions
- **Optional integration**: `PreferencesService` is injected via `setPreferencesService()` rather than a required constructor param. Existing code that doesn't set it continues working unchanged.
- **Defaults allow everything**: When no preferences exist for a user, `shouldSendEmail()` returns true. Users must explicitly opt out. This prevents breaking existing notification flows.
- **`suppressed` flag on records**: Rather than silently dropping notifications, suppressed ones are still recorded with `suppressed: true` so the system has an audit trail.
- **userId lookup via metadata**: The `send()` method extracts `userId` from `payload.metadata.userId` to look up preferences. Event handlers already pass userId in metadata.

### Files Changed
- `src/models/user-preferences.ts` — new file (model + defaults)
- `src/models/notification.ts` — added `suppressed` field to `NotificationRecord`
- `src/repositories/preferences.repository.ts` — new file
- `src/services/preferences.service.ts` — new file
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — added preferences check in `send()`, `setPreferencesService()`
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/services/preferences.service.test.ts` — new file (19 tests)
- `tests/services/notification-preferences.test.ts` — new file (5 tests)

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `NotificationRecord` type now has a required `suppressed: boolean` field. If any code creates `NotificationRecord` objects manually, it must include this field.
- To use preferences, you must: (1) create a `PreferencesRepository` with a `Database`, (2) create a `PreferencesService` with the repo, (3) call `notificationService.setPreferencesService(preferencesService)`. Without step 3, all notifications are sent as before.
- The `shouldSendEmail()` method only checks the email channel. When adding webhook/in-app channels, similar `shouldSend*` methods will be needed.
- Frequency values `'daily-digest'` and `'weekly-digest'` are modeled but not yet implemented in terms of batching/scheduling — they currently just pass through as "allowed to send". A digest system would need to intercept these.
- All 120 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

## Agent 2 — Email Adapter Full Implementation (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Replaced stub EmailAdapter** with full implementation in `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts`:
   - **Template rendering**: Built-in HTML + text templates for all 5 notification types (`order-confirmation`, `order-status-update`, `user-welcome`, `user-updated`, `system-alert`). HTML templates include proper structure and styling; text templates are plain-text equivalents.
   - **HTML escaping**: `escapeHtml()` utility prevents XSS in rendered templates.
   - **Recipient resolution**: `resolveRecipient()` validates email format, normalizes to lowercase, trims whitespace. Returns `null` for invalid addresses.
   - **Pluggable transport**: `EmailTransport` interface for swappable delivery backends. `InMemoryTransport` is the default (for testing). Inject a real transport (SendGrid, SES, SMTP) via constructor.
   - **Error handling**: `send()` never throws — catches transport errors and returns `{ success: false }`.
   - **Enhanced `SentEmail` record**: Now includes `htmlBody`, `notificationType`, and `messageId` fields.
2. **Added `RenderedEmail` type** — intermediate representation between template and transport.
3. **Added 24 unit tests** in `tests/adapters/email.adapter.test.ts`:
   - Recipient validation (valid, lowercase, trim, empty, invalid format)
   - Template rendering for all 5 notification types
   - HTML escaping (XSS prevention)
   - Successful send flow
   - Failure on invalid recipient (no email stored)
   - Failure on transport error (Error and non-Error throws)
   - Custom transport injection
   - Message ID uniqueness
   - `getSentEmails` / `clearSentEmails` behavior
   - `InMemoryTransport` sequential IDs
4. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts` — exports `InMemoryTransport`, `RenderedEmail`, `EmailTransport`
5. **Fixed existing test** in `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — "should deliver via registered adapter" test now uses a valid email address (previously used `user-1` as recipient which the new validation rejects)

### Key Design Decisions
- **Validation at adapter boundary**: The adapter validates email format because it's the system boundary for email delivery. Non-email recipients (e.g., user IDs) should be resolved to email addresses *before* reaching the adapter (in the NotificationService handlers).
- **Transport injection over configuration**: Used constructor injection for the transport layer rather than config-based switching. Makes testing trivial and production wiring explicit.
- **Templates are code, not files**: Templates are defined as functions in the adapter module rather than external template files. Appropriate for this codebase size; can be extracted later if needed.

### Files Changed
- `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts` — full rewrite (stub → real implementation)
- `src/index.ts` — added new exports
- `tests/adapters/email.adapter.test.ts` — new test file (24 tests)
- `tests/services/notification.service.test.ts` — fixed one test to use valid email

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `order:status-changed` handler in `NotificationService` passes `event.order.userId` as the email recipient. This will fail validation since user IDs aren't email addresses. If you're building webhook/in-app adapters, they may accept non-email recipients — but the email adapter requires a valid email address.
- The `EmailTransport` interface is the extension point for real email delivery. To add production email support, implement `EmailTransport` and pass it to the `EmailAdapter` constructor.
- All 96 tests pass, TypeScript compiles clean.

## Agent 1 — Notification System Foundation (Complete)

### What I Did
1. **Chose event-driven architecture** (EventBus pattern) — aligned with CLAUDE.md's stated preference
2. **Defined core notification types** in `src/models/notification.ts`:
   - `NotificationChannel`: `'email' | 'webhook' | 'in-app'`
   - `NotificationType`: `'order-confirmation' | 'order-status-update' | 'user-welcome' | 'user-updated' | 'system-alert'`
   - `NotificationPayload`: what adapters receive for delivery
   - `NotificationResult`: what adapters return after delivery
   - `NotificationRecord`: persisted record after delivery attempt
3. **Created `NotificationAdapter` interface** in `src/adapters/notification.adapter.ts` — all delivery channels must implement this
4. **Created `EmailAdapter`** in `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts` — stub implementation that stores emails in memory
5. **Refactored `NotificationService`** in `src/services/notification.service.ts`:
   - Unified on EventBus (removed CallbackRegistry dependency)
   - Added adapter registration via `registerAdapter()`
   - Public `send(payload)` method for programmatic notification delivery
   - Emits `notification:sent` / `notification:failed` events after delivery
   - Listens to `order:created`, `order:status-changed`, `user:created` via EventBus
6. **Fixed `OrderService`** — now emits `order:status-changed` via EventBus instead of CallbackRegistry
7. **Added notification events** to `src/events/event-types.ts`: `NotificationSentEvent`, `NotificationFailedEvent`
8. **Updated barrel exports** in `src/index.ts`
9. **Updated all tests** — 72 tests passing, TypeScript compiles clean

### Architectural Constraints (ALL agents must follow)
- **All notifications go through EventBus** — no direct service-to-service calls, no CallbackRegistry for notifications
- **All delivery adapters must implement `NotificationAdapter` interface** (`src/adapters/notification.adapter.ts`)
- **Register adapters via `notificationService.registerAdapter(adapter)`** before events fire
- **One adapter per channel** — second registration replaces the first
- **Adapters must not throw** — return `{ success: false }` in `NotificationResult` instead
- **Adapters must not emit events or store records** — that's the NotificationService's job

### Key Files
- `src/models/notification.ts` — types
- `src/adapters/notification.adapter.ts` — adapter interface
- `src/adapters/email.adapter.ts` — email adapter (stub)
- `src/services/notification.service.ts` — orchestrator
- `src/events/event-types.ts` — includes NotificationSentEvent, NotificationFailedEvent

### Warnings for Other Agents
- The `CallbackRegistry` (`src/utils/callback-registry.ts`) still exists but is **no longer used for notifications**. It's still exported from the codebase — don't re-introduce it for notification purposes.
- `NotificationService` auto-subscribes to events in its constructor. If you create a new service that emits events the notification system should react to, add a handler in `NotificationService.registerHandlers()`.
- To add a new notification channel: (1) add to `NotificationChannel` type, (2) create adapter implementing `NotificationAdapter`, (3) register it with NotificationService.
- The `Notification` interface that used to be exported from `notification.service.ts` has been replaced by `NotificationRecord` from `src/models/notification.ts`. If any code imports the old `Notification` type, update it.
